q: What is a Git branch, really?
a: A movable pointer (a ~40-byte ref) to a commit. Creating one doesn't copy files — it just labels a commit — which is why branching is cheap and central to Git.

q: What is a commit, and what links commits into history?
a: An immutable snapshot of tracked files plus metadata and a pointer to its parent(s). Each has a content-derived hash, so commits form a chain/DAG and changing the past changes all later hashes.

q: What does HEAD point to?
a: The branch you're currently on (the attach point for your next commit) — or directly to a commit in 'detached HEAD' state.

q: Name the three areas a change passes through and the commands between them.
a: Working directory --git add--> staging area (index) --git commit--> repository (.git). git restore/checkout brings repo content back to the working dir.

q: What determines whether `git merge` does a fast-forward vs. a three-way merge?
a: Whether the target branch moved since you branched. If it didn't, Git just slides the pointer forward (fast-forward, linear). If both diverged, it creates a merge commit with two parents (three-way).

q: What does `git merge --squash` do?
a: Combines all of a branch's work into a single set of staged changes (which you then commit) — one clean commit, no merge link to the source branch kept.

q: How does rebase differ from merge structurally?
a: Merge adds a two-parent merge commit (non-destructive, preserves history). Rebase replays your commits onto a new base as NEW commits with new hashes, producing a linear history.

q: State the Golden Rule of Rebasing.
a: Never rebase commits others may have already pulled (anything pushed to a shared branch). Rebase changes hashes, so shared rebasing causes duplicate commits and painful conflicts. Rebase only local, unpushed work.
hint: It's about shared vs. private history.

q: What does `git pull --rebase` do and why use it?
a: Instead of fetch+merge, it fetches then replays YOUR local commits on top of the updated branch — avoiding noisy little merge commits. Safe because it only rebases your own unpushed commits.

q: What core trade-off do all branching strategies try to manage?
a: Long-lived branches drift apart and cause painful merges; short-lived branches integrate cheaply but require discipline and strong automation. Each strategy is a different balance point.

q: Describe GitHub Flow.
a: main is always deployable; branch off main per change; open a PR with review + CI; merge to main and deploy. Simple, PR-centric — a great default for web/SaaS teams.

q: What is trunk-based development and what makes it work?
a: Everyone integrates into a single trunk (main) at least daily via tiny short-lived branches; unfinished work hides behind feature flags. It needs strong automated tests, small reviewed PRs, and flag hygiene. It underpins elite CI/CD.

q: When is Git Flow (develop + release/* + hotfix/*) a good fit?
a: Packaged/versioned software with scheduled releases and multiple supported versions (installed apps, libraries, firmware). It's heavy for continuously-delivered web software.

q: How do feature flags enable trunk-based development?
a: They let you merge unfinished code to main disabled behind a flag, decoupling deploy (code is live) from release (feature is visible). Enables gradual rollouts, A/B tests, and kill-switches — at the cost of flag lifecycle management.

q: What does `git commit --amend` do, and what's the caveat?
a: Replaces the last commit with a new one (new hash) — to fix its message or add staged changes. Caveat: don't amend a commit already pushed to a shared branch (Golden Rule).

q: What is interactive rebase used for?
a: git rebase -i lets you rewrite a series of commits — reorder, reword, squash/fixup, edit, or drop them — e.g. to squash 'wip/typo' commits into one clean commit before a PR. Private history only.

q: Compare git reset --soft, --mixed, and --hard.
a: All move the branch pointer. --soft keeps changes staged; --mixed (default) keeps them unstaged; --hard discards working-dir changes entirely (destructive — no recycle bin for uncommitted work).

q: reset vs revert — which undoes a commit on shared main, and why?
a: Use revert: it adds a NEW commit that applies the inverse, preserving history (safe for shared branches). reset moves the pointer back, rewriting history — only safe on private branches.
hint: One rewrites history, one adds to it.

q: What is the reflog and why does it make history-editing safe?
a: A log of every position HEAD has been (kept ~90 days), including commits 'lost' to reset/rebase/amend. `git reflog` finds them, so you can point a branch back at almost any lost work.

q: What does `git cherry-pick` do?
a: Applies a single commit (or range) from another branch onto your current branch as a new commit — e.g. backporting one fix to a release branch.

q: What's the difference between git fetch and git pull?
a: fetch is safe/read-only: it downloads remote changes and updates origin/* refs without touching your files. pull = fetch + integrate (merge or rebase) into your current branch.

q: What are the steps to resolve a merge conflict?
a: 1) git status to see conflicted files; 2) edit each to the desired final result, removing the <<<<<<< ======= >>>>>>> markers; 3) git add the file; 4) git merge --continue (or rebase --continue). --abort bails out.

q: What does `git bisect` do?
a: Binary-searches history between a known-good and known-bad commit to pinpoint the commit that introduced a bug — ~log2(n) tests. Automatable with `git bisect run ./test.sh` (uses exit code).

q: What problem does `git stash` solve?
a: It shelves your uncommitted changes (optionally untracked with -u) onto a stack so you can switch contexts with a clean working dir, then `git stash pop` to restore them later.

q: What does `git worktree` let you do?
a: Check out another branch in a separate directory sharing the same repo — work on a hotfix or review a PR without stashing or disturbing your current branch.
